questions.stem: What are the two essential parts of every recursive function?
options: A base case (stopping condition) and a recursive case (calling itself with a smaller input)
A return value and a print statement
questions.explanation: Every recursive function needs: (1) a base case that returns a value without recursing, and (2) a recursive case that breaks the problem into smaller pieces and calls itself.

questions.stem: What error does Python raise when recursion goes too deep?
questions.explanation: Python limits recursion to ~1000 calls (configurable via sys.setrecursionlimit). Exceeding this raises RecursionError to prevent the call stack from consuming all available memory.

questions.stem: Naive recursive Fibonacci is exponentially slow. The fix is:
options: Memoisation — caching results so each subproblem is computed only once
questions.explanation: fib(n) recomputes fib(n-2) for every branch. With memoisation (@functools.cache or @lru_cache), each value is computed exactly once, reducing O(2ⁿ) to O(n).

questions.stem: What does `@functools.lru_cache(maxsize=None)` do on a recursive function?
options: Caches return values so repeated calls with the same arguments return instantly
Limits how many times it can recurse
questions.explanation: lru_cache stores (args → result) pairs. On a subsequent call with the same args, it returns the cached result instead of recomputing. For recursive functions this avoids exponential recomputation.

questions.stem: When is recursion MORE natural than iteration?
options: When the problem involves tree/graph structures or nested data of unknown depth
When the input is always small
questions.explanation: Recursion maps naturally onto tree-shaped problems: file systems, HTML DOMs, JSON objects, and abstract syntax trees. The recursive structure of the code mirrors the recursive structure of the data.

questions.stem: What is the purpose of the accumulator pattern in recursion?
options: To count how many times the function has been called
To carry intermediate results as a parameter, avoiding the need to multiply/add on the way back up the call stack
To increase performance by skipping computation
questions.explanation: In `fact_tail(n, acc=1)`, instead of `n * fact(n-1)` (which needs to wait for the return), we pass the accumulated result as `acc`, making the recursive call the last operation (tail call).
